title: CSR: Surgery & Procedures (Scheduling & Client Care)
description: A CSR course on surgical scheduling, pre-surgical instructions, intake paperwork, consent forms, estimates, fasting status, communication boundaries, and discharge coordination.

blocks.prompt: A client asks if their senior pet is safe for anesthesia.

options.text: Guarantee safety.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Route the medical question to the veterinarian/medical team.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Say anesthesia is always dangerous and scare them.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: The CSR supports surgery by scheduling correctly, providing approved instructions, confirming forms/estimates, and preparing the handoff. The CSR does not make anesthesia or medical-clearance decisions.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

blocks.question: What is the CSR surgery role?

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Accurate logistics, forms, instructions, and routing.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.back: Support flow; do not make medical decisions.

blocks.content: Future media: use a purposeful photo, diagram, role-play video, or checklist only when it improves learning; avoid decorative filler.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

blocks.prompt: A diabetic pet owner asks about insulin before surgery.

options.text: Guess based on usual routine.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Route to the medical team according to protocol.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Tell all owners to skip all meds.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: Pre-surgical calls may include arrival time, fasting instructions, approved medication instructions, estimate reminders, forms, and what to expect. Medication questions must follow medical guidance.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Approved logistics and instructions.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.content: Future media: use a purposeful photo, diagram, role-play video, or checklist only when it improves learning; avoid decorative filler.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

blocks.prompt: The client says the pet vomited overnight before surgery.

options.text: Ignore it because surgery is scheduled.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Alert the medical team and document/route the concern.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Promise surgery will happen anyway.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: Day-of surgery check-in should confirm patient/client identity, procedure, consent, estimate, fasting status if required, contact number, and any new concerns. New symptoms should be routed before routine flow continues.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

blocks.question: What should be confirmed at surgical check-in?

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Identity, procedure, forms, estimate, fasting/status, contact, and new concerns.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.content: Future media: use a purposeful photo, diagram, role-play video, or checklist only when it improves learning; avoid decorative filler.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

blocks.prompt: A client asks if the pet will definitely be okay.

options.text: Guarantee a perfect outcome.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Acknowledge concern and route the update request appropriately.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Tell them not to call.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: Clients are anxious during procedures. The CSR can explain general process and route update requests but should not interpret medical status or guarantee outcomes. At discharge, the CSR supports scheduling, payment, printed materials, and handoff.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

blocks.question: What should CSRs avoid during surgery updates?

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Medical interpretation and outcome guarantees.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.back: Coordinate logistics and follow the medical team’s instructions.

blocks.content: Future media: use a purposeful photo, diagram, role-play video, or checklist only when it improves learning; avoid decorative filler.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.
